mnemonics.title: Scheduling Criteria记忆法
mnemonics.content: CPU UTILIZATION: KEEP CPU BUSY | THROUGHPUT: JOBS PER TIME | TURNAROUND: SUBMIT TO COMPLETE | WAITING: READY QUEUE TIME | RESPONSE: FIRST RESPONSE TIME

mnemonics.title: Scheduling Algorithms记忆法
mnemonics.content: FCFS: FIRST COME FIRST SERVED | SJF: SHORTEST JOB FIRST | RR: ROUND ROBIN | PRIORITY: HIGH PRIORITY FIRST

acronyms.usage: Scheduling algorithm that selects shortest burst time process
Standard thread API for UNIX systems

visualTips.visualization: Imagine ONE BUS (kernel thread) carrying MULTIPLE PASSENGERS (user threads). If bus breaks down, all passengers stuck.
Imagine ONE-ONE MAPPING: Each person has their own car. Expensive but flexible - can go anywhere independently.
Imagine POOL OF CARS (kernel threads) shared by POOL OF PASSENGERS (user threads). Efficient car utilization.
Mix of dedicated cars and shared pool - some important passengers get own cars, others share.
Think of a FERRIS WHEEL - each person gets equal ride time, then next person. Cyclic rotation.
Think of PRIORITY QUEUE at checkout - shortest line served first regardless of arrival order.

analogies.analogy: Like WORKERS (user threads) and MANAGERS (kernel threads). Workers do actual work, managers coordinate with higher-ups (OS).
Like GROUP PROJECT where one person does all communication with professor. If that person is absent, whole team stuck.
Like CHECKOUT COUNTERS in supermarket. Load balancing ensures no line gets too long; affinity keeps regular customer with favorite cashier.
Like TURNS in a board game - each player gets fixed time to play, then passes to next.
Like a restaurant where VIP customers always get served first - regular customers may never get fed if VIPs keep coming.
Like a long truck blocking a highway - many small cars stuck behind one slow vehicle, even though they could finish faster.
